focus-stacking-calculator.beginner: Focus stacking is a technique where you take multiple photos at different focus distances and merge them in software to create an image with front-to-back sharpness. It is essential for macro photography, where depth of field is razor-thin, and useful in landscape photography when you need both near foreground and distant background in sharp focus.

0.heading: Why Stacking Is Necessary
0.text: At macro magnifications, even f/16 may yield only a few millimeters of depth of field. Stopping down further introduces diffraction softening that degrades the image. Focus stacking lets you shoot at the lens's sharpest aperture (typically f/5.6-f/8) and combine the sharp slices. Each frame shifts focus slightly forward, covering a new slice of the subject.

1.heading: Planning the Stack
1.text: The key to a successful stack is ensuring each shot's depth of field overlaps the next by 20-30%. Too little overlap creates gaps where nothing is sharp; too much wastes frames without benefit. The calculator determines the per-shot DoF using the circle of confusion formula, subtracts the overlap margin, and divides the total depth range by the effective step to compute the number of shots.

2.heading: Merging & Alignment
2.text: Software like Photoshop, Helicon Focus, or Zerene Stacker aligns the frames (slight magnification shifts occur as focus changes), then selects the sharpest pixels from each layer. Shooting on a sturdy tripod with consistent lighting and using a focus rail for precise increments produces the cleanest results.

0.label: Aperture
0.description: Determines per-shot depth of field. A moderate aperture (f/5.6-f/8) balances sharpness and DoF per frame. Avoid very small apertures — diffraction reduces the sharpness you are stacking for.

1.label: Depth Range
1.description: The distance from the nearest to farthest point you want sharp. Wider ranges require more shots. In macro work this may be just centimeters; in landscapes it can span meters to infinity.

2.label: Overlap
2.description: The percentage of each shot's DoF that overlaps the adjacent shots. 20-30% is standard. Less risks gaps; more wastes frames.

0.text: Use a focus rail instead of the lens's focus ring for macro stacking. A rail moves the entire camera forward in precise increments, avoiding the slight magnification changes that occur when refocusing the lens.

1.text: Keep your aperture at the lens's sweet spot (typically f/5.6-f/8). The point of stacking is to avoid stopping down to f/16+, so use the sharpest aperture and let the stack handle depth.

2.text: For landscape stacks, shoot front-to-back: focus on the nearest element first, then step focus toward infinity. This makes alignment easier since the foreground (with the most parallax) is captured first.

focalLength.definition: The lens focal length in millimeters. Longer focal lengths reduce per-shot depth of field, requiring more frames to cover the same depth range.

aperture.definition: The lens opening (f-number). For stacking, use the lens's sharpest aperture rather than stopping down — the stack provides the depth.

sensor.definition: Larger sensors have a shallower per-shot depth of field at equivalent framing, which may increase the number of required stack frames.

nearLimit.definition: The closest point in the scene that you want sharp. This is where the first shot in the stack will be focused.

farLimit.definition: The farthest point in the scene that you want sharp. The last shot in the stack will cover this distance.

overlap.definition: The percentage of depth-of-field overlap between consecutive shots. 20-30% ensures no gaps after software alignment and merging.

shotCount.definition: The total number of frames needed to cover the depth range with the specified overlap. More shots = more processing but smoother results.

0.scenario: You are photographing a small flower that is 3cm deep from front petal to back stem. Your macro lens at f/8 gives about 4mm of DoF per shot. Roughly how many frames do you need with 25% overlap?
0.hint: Each shot covers 4mm, but 25% overlap means the effective step is 3mm per frame. Divide 30mm by 3mm.
0.successMessage: Correct! With 4mm DoF and 25% overlap, each step advances 3mm. 30mm / 3mm = 10 shots to cover the entire flower.
0.failureMessage: With 25% overlap, each frame's effective step is 75% of the DoF: 4mm × 0.75 = 3mm. You need 30mm / 3mm = 10 shots.

1.scenario: You want the sharpest possible result from your focus stack. Should you shoot each frame at f/2.8, f/8, or f/22?
1.hint: Consider the lens's sharpest aperture and how diffraction affects small apertures.
1.successMessage: Right! f/8 is typically the sharpest aperture for most lenses. f/2.8 has too little DoF per shot (wasting frames) and f/22 suffers from diffraction softening.
1.failureMessage: f/8 is the best choice. f/2.8 gives very thin per-shot DoF (requiring many more frames), and f/22 introduces diffraction softening that defeats the purpose of stacking for sharpness.

2.scenario: After merging your focus stack, you notice a soft band across the middle of the image where nothing is sharp. What most likely went wrong?
2.hint: Think about what connects one frame's sharp zone to the next.
2.successMessage: Exactly! Insufficient overlap between adjacent frames creates a gap where neither shot is sharp. Increase overlap to 25-30% and reshoot.
2.failureMessage: A soft band usually means the gap between focus steps was too large — the DoF of adjacent frames did not overlap enough. Increase the overlap percentage.

2.label: The tripod moved during the sequence
